title: Making a simple leather shoulder strap
subtitle: Cut, edge, and finish a plain leather bag strap with roller buckle fittings
excerpt: How to cut a flat leather shoulder strap from a vegetable-tanned side, bevel and burnish the edges, punch the buckle holes, and attach the hardware. Covers strap cutting without a strap cutter, edge finishing, and hardware attachment.
sourceNotes: Leather Working Guild publications (open access). Al Stohlman Leathercraft instruction series (public domain editions). Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

glossaryTerms.definition: A hand-stitching method for leather using two needles threaded on a single length of waxed thread, each needle working from one side of the piece simultaneously. Each stitch is locked from both sides, making the seam stronger and more durable than machine stitching. If one stitch breaks, the others remain intact. The stitching rhythm is: right needle through the hole first, left needle through behind the right thread, pull both needles through until the stitch is firm.

content.text: A flat shoulder strap is a manageable first leather project: straight cuts, consistent edge work, and hand stitching at the buckle ends using the

. Use vegetable-tanned leather of 3 to 4 mm thickness for a strap that is firm but not stiff. Chrome-tanned leather at the same thickness is softer and more supple but does not take edge burnishing as well.

Vegetable-tanned leather, 3 to 4 mm thickness, at least 30 mm wide × the required strap length plus 200 mm for folds at the hardware ends. Two roller buckles or swivel snap hooks to fit the strap width. Waxed thread. Two blunt-tip harness needles. A stitching chisels or pricking iron (or a sharp awl for marking stitch holes). Edge finish wax or gum tragacanth for burnishing.

Mark the strap width on the flesh side of the leather using a combination square and carpenters pencil. Score along the marked line with the leatherwork knife, pressing firmly against a metal straightedge with the cutting edge held vertical. A leather worker's knife cuts cleanly in one firm pass; multiple light passes produce a ragged edge. For a finished strap width of 25 mm, cut two parallel lines 25 mm apart along the full length of the hide strip.
Step 2, bevel and burnish the edges

Run the edge beveller along all four long edges of the strap, both faces, both sides, removing a thin triangle of leather from each corner. Use the No. 2 or No. 3 size beveller for a 3 to 4 mm leather. After bevelling, sand the edges progressively through 120, 180, and 240 grit using the sanding block, holding the strap flat on the bench and working along the edge in long strokes. Dampen the edge slightly with water, apply edge wax or gum tragacanth, and burnish by rubbing vigorously with a wood slicker or the smooth back of a tool handle until the edge is smooth and slightly glossy.
Step 3, punch stitch holes and saddle-stitch the hardware

Fold the last 50 mm of the strap around the bar of the roller buckle or through the swivel snap. Mark the stitch line on the folded end using the utility knife scored lightly along a stitching groover or ruler, 4 mm in from the edge. Punch the stitch holes with a stitching chisel or pricking iron at 3 mm spacing. Thread two needles on a single length of waxed thread four times the stitch length and saddle-stitch the folded end, locking each stitch as described. Finish with two back-stitches and trim the thread.

Mark a row of adjustment holes at the buckle end of the strap: five holes at 25 mm intervals is standard. Punch each hole with a rotary or drive punch matched to the buckle tongue width. Apply a final coat of leather conditioner or neatsfoot oil to the full strap surface to restore flexibility after the edge sanding.

attrs.symptom: Cut edges are ragged or angled rather than square
attrs.cause: The knife was not held vertical against the straightedge, or the blade was not sharp enough to cut in one pass.
attrs.fix: Strop the blade to a keen edge. On the next cut, press the blade firmly against the metal ruler and maintain the knife at exactly 90 degrees to the leather surface throughout the stroke.

attrs.symptom: Saddle-stitch pulls out of alignment at one end of the stitch run
attrs.cause: The stitch holes were not punched perpendicular to the leather surface, causing the thread to enter at an angle.
attrs.fix: Always punch holes with the chisel held vertical; use a small mallet and a solid bench support beneath the work.
